text: Three-dimensional (3D) organic–inorganic lead halide perovskites have become one of the most studied semiconductor materials for the realization of efficient and low-cost optoelectronic devices such as solar cells and optoelectronic memories, among others. Interest in derivatives has recently increased and 2D halide perovskites constitute one of the subclasses of perovskite semiconductors with the potential to provide deeper understanding of the structural and chemical physics of the perovskite family of materials. The layered structure, which is responsible for the two-dimensional nature of these materials, provides a hybrid organic–inorganic interface that leads to peculiar physical properties. In addition, mixed dimensional heterostructures based on the intrinsic complementarity between 3D and 2D dimensionalities can be exploited to compensate the shortcomings of individual materials. This review is aimed at understanding how the structural differences between 3D and 2D perovskites affect their properties and at illustrating the solution-based synthetic approaches for preparing mixed dimensional semiconductors and heterostructures. The influence of 3D–2D mixtures in solar cells performance and stability is also deeply discussed. Finally, the possibility of extending the applications of such materials beyond photovoltaics is explored, providing a perspective on mixed dimensional perovskites as tunable semiconductor materials suitable for realising highly efficient and stable optoelectronic devices.
